title: Treating red mite in a chicken coop
subtitle: Finding, killing, and preventing the return of Dermanyssus gallinae in a small backyard coop.
excerpt: Red mite live in coop crevices during the day and feed on sleeping hens at night. A bad infestation causes anaemia, stress, and a sharp drop in egg production. In summer they can reach population levels of millions within a few weeks. The treatment cycle must address both the mites in the structure and the source of reinfestation.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002). https://www.gov.uk
British Hen Welfare Trust red mite guidance. https://www.bhwt.org.uk

content.text: (Dermanyssus gallinae) lives in the structural joints of the coop during daylight and emerges at night to feed on sleeping hens. A coop that is clean in April can be heavily infested by July. The diagnostic clue is a grey powder in the joints and crevices of the perch and coop walls: this is mite faeces and shed skins. Draw a white piece of card along a perch joint at night and check it under a torch; red-coloured mites on the card confirm the problem.

attrs.body: Check several birds for pale wattles, especially the lowest-ranked hens. They cannot push for perch space away from the worst of the mites. Hens that refuse to enter the coop at dusk, when they would normally roost happily, is a strong sign that the coop is infested.
attrs.title: Signs of severe infestation

content.text: Move the birds out of the coop before treating. Remove all bedding and burn it or seal it in bags for collection. Jet-wash every surface, joint, and crevice in the coop structure. Pay particular attention to the joints on perch ends, the undersides of perches, and any rough-edged wood where mites hide during the day. Leave the coop to dry fully before applying treatment: wet surfaces dilute and neutralise most products.

Two kinds of treatment are sold without a vet prescription for backyard hens. The first is diatomaceous earth, a physical drying powder. The second is licensed spray products. For a moderate or bad infestation, a licensed spray is more reliable than diatomaceous earth alone. Spray every surface and let it dry fully before the birds return. Follow the label for re-entry times, usually four to twelve hours.

Repeat treatment after seven days to catch eggs that hatched since the first treatment. A single treatment never eliminates an established infestation: the mite cycle from egg to adult is 7 to 10 days and the first treatment targets adults only.
The most common way mites come back is on new birds from an unknown flock. Wild birds on the coop roof are the second.

in several birds after a clean coop check means the mites have returned. Dust the perch ends and nest box corners with diatomaceous earth each month through summer as a barrier. Do the white-card check every month. Catching a small infestation early is far easier than dealing with a bad one.
